85:["$","p",null,{"style":{"fontSize":"1rem","color":"var(--text-body)","lineHeight":1.75,"marginBottom":"1.25em","marginTop":0},"children":[["$","strong",null,{"style":{"color":"var(--text-strong)","fontWeight":600},"children":["Advantage Actor-Critic (",["$","$L2b",null,{"term":"A2C"}],")"]}]," applies the actor-critic update synchronously: all\nparallel workers complete their rollouts, gradients are aggregated, and a single\nsynchronized update is applied. 86:["$","p",null,{"style":{"fontSize":"1rem","color":"var(--text-body)","lineHeight":1.75,"marginBottom":"1.25em","marginTop":0},"children":[["$","strong",null,{"style":{"color":"var(--text-strong)","fontWeight":600},"children":["Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic (",["$","$L2b",null,{"term":"A3C"}],")"]}]," runs workers asynchronously — each\nworker pushes gradient updates to a shared parameter server without waiting for others.\nThe asynchrony decorrelates experience across workers (similar to a replay buffer) but\nwithout requiring off-policy corrections, since each worker runs the current policy.\n",["$","$L2b",null,{"term":"A2C"}]," is generally preferred in practice because synchronous updates produce more stable\nlearning — the additional variance from asynchronous gradient staleness typically\noutweighs the decorrelation benefit."]}]

9e:["$","p",null,{"style":{"fontSize":"1rem","color":"var(--text-body)","lineHeight":1.75,"marginBottom":"1.25em","marginTop":0},"children":["In supervised learning, a large gradient step may hurt performance but the loss function\nremains valid — we can detect the mistake and recover.
